name: Maero
description: The Maero are a race of wild, man-like creatures from Māori mythology. They are generally described as being large, muscular, and covered in long, coarse hair. Known for their powerful build and aggressive nature, Maero are said to possess long, sharp fingernails which they use as tools and weapons. They are often depicted as cannibals and are feared for their violent tendencies. Some accounts suggest that they are skilled hunters and trappers, using their strength and cunning to capture prey. They live in remote, forested areas, far from human settlements, and are rarely seen. Stories of the Maero serve as warnings about the dangers of the wilderness and the importance of respecting the natural world. Legends vary across different iwi (tribes), with some accounts portraying them as entirely monstrous, while others suggest they were simply a reclusive and misunderstood people.
subtitle: Wild, Hairy Cannibals of the Forest
appearance: Maero are typically described as large, powerfully built humanoids covered in thick, dark hair. Their skin is often described as being rough and leathery. They possess exceptionally long and sharp fingernails, sometimes described as claws, which they use for digging, hunting, and combat. Their eyes are said to be small and beady, and their faces are often obscured by their long hair. They are often depicted as being perpetually scowling or having a fierce expression.
habitat: Remote, dense forests and mountainous regions, far from human settlements. They prefer areas with ample game and natural resources.
behavior: Maero are generally depicted as solitary and aggressive creatures. They are fiercely territorial and will attack anyone who encroaches on their domain. They are known for their cunning and their ability to set traps for unsuspecting travelers. Cannibalism is a recurring theme in Maero legends. They are said to be skilled hunters and trappers, using their strength and cunning to capture prey. They avoid contact with humans whenever possible.

keyMyths.title: Maero and the Lost Traveler
keyMyths.description: A lone traveler becomes lost in the forest and stumbles upon the lair of a Maero. The Maero attempts to capture and eat the traveler, but the traveler uses their wits to escape.

sources.author: Sir George Grey
sources.work: Polynesian Mythology and Ancient Traditional History of the New Zealand Race
sources.description: A collection of Māori myths and legends, including some accounts of the Maero.
sources.text: A collection of Māori myths and legends, including some accounts of the Maero.

sources.author: Margaret Orbell
sources.work: The Illustrated Encyclopedia of Māori Myth and Legend
sources.description: A comprehensive overview of Māori mythology, with entries on various creatures and deities, including the Maero.
